---
title: "Working Papers"
---

Drafts available on request.

::: {.pub-entry}
[Scarcity, Worthiness, and the Politics of Refugee Admission in the Global North]{.pub-title}\
[R&R, *The Journal of Politics*.]{.pub-venue}

[Examines why some refugee crises prompt generosity while others trigger restriction. Develops a formal model incorporating scarcity costs, refugee racial identity, and geopolitical salience, and tests predictions using country-year asylum policy data.]{.pub-abstract}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[The Discretionary Color Line in International Institutions]{.pub-title}

[Tests whether formally equal rules eliminate racial hierarchy in practice, using the Schengen visa regime as a case study. Ancestral distance predicts visa refusal rates, with busier consulates discriminating more.]{.pub-abstract}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[Traces of the Past: Regime Histories and Anti-Foreigner Violence in Post-Unification Germany]{.pub-title}\
[with Hannah M. Alarian and Michael Bernhard.]{.pub-venue}

[Investigates whether distinct regime histories explain disparate patterns of anti-foreigner violence in post-unification Germany, examining the impact of East–West "coming to terms with the past" approaches.]{.pub-abstract}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[Who Deserves to Belong? Public Opinion and U.S. Naturalization Decisions]{.pub-title}\
[with Juliana K. Mucci and Hannah M. Alarian.]{.pub-venue}

[Citizenship is one of the most important and politically sensitive policy issues in democracies worldwide. Naturalization is a crucial aspect of democratic inclusion, as it requires states and their citizens to accept immigrants as fellow citizens. American attitudes toward immigration, in particular, are well-established, with Americans often favoring immigrants from culturally similar origins or those who hold high-status positions. Yet, little is known about Americans' preferences for naturalization. This article addresses this gap by examining Americans' evaluations of United States (US) citizenship applicants. Supplementing an existing study ([osf.io/5ctkj](https://osf.io/5ctkj)), we test this theory in another original conjoint experiment to isolate attitudes toward naturalization in the US. We hypothesize that the "hidden consensus" that has been found to exist across party lines regarding immigration attitudes does not hold in the context of naturalization attitudes, especially in a polarized political climate. This study provides a further nuanced understanding of how Americans' own political identities shape their attitudes toward citizenship.]{.pub-abstract}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[National Pride and Prejudice: How National Identity Moderates Anti-Immigrant Attitudes]{.pub-title}\
[with Hannah M. Alarian.]{.pub-venue}

[Survey experiments testing whether connecting immigration to the NHS reduces anti-immigrant sentiment among strong national identifiers.]{.pub-abstract}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[Immigration Does Not Lower Social Capital]{.pub-title}

[Uses county-level data and an instrumental variable design to challenge the assumption that immigration diminishes social capital.]{.pub-abstract}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[Races at the Bottom: Local Impacts of Official Discrimination on Election Outcomes]{.pub-title}\
[with Conor O'Dwyer and Matthew Stenberg.]{.pub-venue}

[Analyzes Polish "LGBT Free Zones" and their impact on Law and Justice party election outcomes in 2024 municipal elections.]{.pub-abstract}
:::

::: {.pub-entry}
[Voting for Extremists: How Extremist Protests Influence Far-Right Voting Behavior in Germany]{.pub-title}\
[with Hannah M. Alarian and Sebastian Elischer.]{.pub-venue}

[Examines AfD proximity to right-wing extremist organizations and its electoral impact across election cycles 2013–2021.]{.pub-abstract}
:::
